While AI dramatically increases code output volume, Orosz argues that meaningful improvements in productivity, reliability, and simplicity remain unimpressive so far, suggesting AI has not yet qualified as a true silver bullet for software engineering.

item.title: What is Code
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Unmesh Joshi, via Martin Fowler's site, argues that code serves dual purposes — machine instructions and a conceptual model of the problem domain — and uses this framework to examine what the rise of LLM-driven agents means for the future of source code. The piece raises whether human-authored source code will remain necessary as AI delegation increases, making it directly relevant to engineers reconsidering the role of programming languages and abstractions in an AI-assisted workflow.

item.title: Thoughts on GitLab's workforce reduction" and "structural and strategic decisions"
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison analyzes GitLab's workforce restructuring announcement, which frames layoffs and org changes explicitly around the "agentic era" of software development. GitLab argues that AI agents will collapse software production costs, triggering a Jevons-paradox-driven explosion in software demand and developer platform value. Willison notes this optimistic view should be taken skeptically, as GitLab's halved stock price suggests the market fears agentic engineering may actually erode their core business.

item.title: Quoting James Shore
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: James Shore argues that AI coding agents only deliver net value if they reduce maintenance costs proportionally to the speed gains they provide — doubling output while holding or increasing maintenance costs actually worsens long-term technical debt. The math means that faster code generation without corresponding maintenance improvements leads to quadrupled costs, not productivity gains. This is a critical framing for engineers evaluating whether AI-assisted workflows genuinely improve sustainability or merely accelerate liability.

item.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights Jason Koebler's concept of the "Zombie Internet" — a state where AI-generated and AI-assisted content has become so pervasive online that it distorts human writing styles and makes authentic content increasingly hard to identify. Unlike the "Dead Internet" (bots talking to bots), the Zombie Internet describes a messy hybrid of humans, AI agents, and AI-assisted users all interacting simultaneously. This matters to software engineers as it frames a growing signal-to-noise problem that affects research, communication, and the broader information ecosystem they operate in.

item.title: Using LLM in the shebang line of a script
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison demonstrates a novel pattern for embedding LLM invocations directly in Unix shebang lines, turning plain text files into executable AI-powered scripts. The technique supports tool calls and YAML-defined Python functions, enabling LLM-driven computation and data querying. This offers software engineers a lightweight, composable way to integrate LLM agents into shell-based workflows without boilerplate code.

item.title: Learning on the Shop floor
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Shopify CEO Tobias Lütke describes River, an internal AI coding agent that operates exclusively in public Slack channels, turning every interaction into a shared learning resource visible to the whole company. Simon Willison draws a parallel to Midjourney's early public Discord model, arguing that forced visibility accelerates skill-building with AI tools through "osmosis learning." For software engineers, this highlights a concrete organizational pattern for scaling AI adoption without formal training programs.

item.title: Quoting New York Times Editors’ Note
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a New York Times editors' note admitting that a quote attributed to Canadian politician Pierre Poilievre was actually an AI-generated summary hallucinated as a direct quotation. The incident underscores a concrete, real-world failure mode of LLM hallucinations — fabricated quotes presented as fact — with implications for anyone integrating AI tools into research or content workflows.

item.title: Oxide and Friends 5/4/2026 -- AI in Computer Science Education
item.author: Bryan Cantrill
item.description: Two Brown CS professors discuss their experimental intro course that heavily incorporates agentic programming, using a "smoke the whole pack" approach to AI exposure. The conversation explores what students actually learn when AI tools are deeply integrated from the start, raising broader questions about how AI is reshaping CS education and what foundational skills still matter for future engineers.

item.title: Using Claude Code: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of HTML
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison reflects on a prompting technique shift: favoring HTML over Markdown as an output format when querying LLMs like Claude, based on an Anthropic engineer's argument that HTML unlocks richer outputs (SVGs, interactive widgets, inline annotations). With modern models no longer constrained by tight token limits, Willison finds HTML-first prompting delivers substantially more navigable and informative responses. This is a practical prompt-engineering insight directly applicable to any engineer using LLMs for code review or technical explanations.

item.title: Pushing Local Models With Focus And Polish
item.author: Armin Ronacher
item.description: Armin Ronacher argues that local AI model inference is held back not by model quality but by fragmentation, poor UX, and lack of focused polish — issues like missing tool-parameter streaming and complex configuration chains. He advocates "picking one winner" and building a tight, product-quality stack around it, exemplified by his pi-ds4 extension that embeds Salvatore Sanfilippo's ds4.c (a Mac-native DeepSeek V4 Flash engine) directly into his coding agent Pi with zero manual configuration.

item.title: llm-gemini 0.31
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison announces the release of llm-gemini 0.31, noting that the Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite model has exited preview and is now generally available. The update is relevant to engineers using the `llm` CLI tool to interact with Google's Gemini models, signaling the model's stabilization for production use.

item.title: Behind the Scenes Hardening Firefox with Claude Mythos Preview
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Mozilla used early access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos model to dramatically accelerate Firefox security hardening, jumping from ~20–30 security bug fixes per month in 2025 to 423 in April 2026. The effort combined more capable models with improved techniques for steering, scaling, and stacking them to generate high-signal vulnerability reports. This marks a notable shift from AI-generated bug reports being dismissed as noise to becoming a serious, productive security research tool.

item.title: The Pulse: AI load breaks GitHub – why not other vendors?
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz examines how an unexpected surge in AI agent activity — requiring a 30x capacity increase by GitHub's own revised estimates — is a key driver behind GitHub's months-long reliability crisis, including data integrity failures and near-daily outages. The piece argues GitHub was caught off guard by AI-driven load growth that competitors like Google had anticipated and planned for well in advance. This matters to software engineers because it illustrates how AI agent adoption is stressing foundational developer infrastructure at a systemic level.

item.title: Notes on the xAI/Anthropic data center deal
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison analyzes Anthropic's deal to use xAI's Colossus 1 data center, raising concerns about its poor environmental record and the supply chain risk of Elon Musk retaining the right to reclaim compute if he deems Anthropic's AI harmful to humanity. He also notes xAI's abrupt two-week deprecation notice for several Grok models, highlighting reliability risks for developers depending on third-party AI infrastructure.

item.title: The Pulse: Did capacity shortages turn Anthropic hostile to devs?
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz examines several AI-industry developments affecting software engineers: Anthropic's possible capacity-driven restrictions on Claude Code, Amazon lifting its ban on Claude Code and Codex, Meta assigning engineers to data-labelling tasks ahead of job cuts, and a trend toward small "AI-forward" teams of 5–10 developers. GitHub's upcoming shift to API token-based pricing for Copilot is flagged as a major, potentially unpredictable cost change for engineering teams.

item.title: Live blog: Code w/ Claude 2026
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison live-blogs Anthropic's "Code w/ Claude" 2026 event, covering the keynote and other sessions throughout the day. The article is relevant to software engineers tracking AI coding tools, as it reports firsthand on Anthropic's latest developments around Claude and Claude Code — Anthropic's AI-powered coding assistant.

item.title: Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison argues that vibe coding and agentic engineering — once clearly distinct practices — are converging in his own workflow as coding agents become reliable enough that even experienced engineers stop reviewing every line. He grapples with the accountability gap this creates (AI has no professional reputation), notes that the entire software development lifecycle was implicitly designed around human coding throughput, and identifies a new evaluation challenge: AI-generated repos with tests and docs are now indistinguishable from carefully handcrafted ones. Despite these concerns, he remains unworried about his career, framing AI as an amplifier of existing expertise rather than a replacement.

item.title: Our AI started a cafe in Stockholm
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison critiques Andon Labs' experiment running an AI agent ("Mona") as a cafe manager in Stockholm, arguing that autonomous AI systems causing real-world disruptions — such as bombarding suppliers with erroneous "EMERGENCY" emails or submitting flawed permit applications to police — are unethical when third parties haven't consented. He calls for mandatory human-in-the-loop oversight for any outbound agent actions that affect people outside the experiment, a key design principle for agentic AI deployments.

item.title: Fragments: May 5
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: This Martin Fowler digest covers several AI-in-engineering topics: an open-source framework (Lattice) that embeds structured engineering disciplines into AI coding workflows; the debate over cloud vs. local models for agentic programming; Google's AI Overview defamation liability; Big Tech's massive AI capex; and Kent Beck's "Genie Tarpit" concern that AI tools accumulate internal quality debt, potentially undermining long-term codebase maintainability.

item.title: datasette-llm 0.1a7
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases datasette-llm 0.1a7, adding support for configuring default options (e.g., model selection and temperature) for LLM-powered enrichment operations within Datasette. This update reflects the maturing plugin architecture for integrating LLMs into data workflows, relevant to engineers building AI-augmented data tooling.

item.title: llm-echo 0.5a0
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases llm-echo 0.5a0, a plugin for the LLM CLI tool that provides a fake "echo" model for automated testing without invoking a real LLM. The new `-o thinking 1` option simulates reasoning/thinking blocks, enabling developers to test workflows that depend on the upcoming LLM 0.32a0 features. This is relevant to engineers building or testing pipelines around LLM tooling and agent architectures.

item.title: Granite 4.1 3B SVG Pelican Gallery
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison evaluates IBM's newly released Apache 2.0-licensed Granite 4.1 3B LLM by prompting 21 quantized GGUF variants to generate an SVG of a pelican riding a bicycle, testing whether model size correlates with output quality. His finding: no meaningful quality difference across sizes — all variants performed poorly. This serves as a practical, if informal, benchmark of quantized model performance for engineers considering local deployment of small LLMs.

item.title: Quoting Andy Masley
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison quotes Andy Masley debunking the "farmland loss" argument against data center construction, noting that the land sold to hyperscalers is negligible compared to the vast acreage farmers have voluntarily sold since 2000. The argument matters to engineers tracking AI infrastructure debates, as it directly addresses a common critique of the physical footprint required to scale AI compute.

item.title: April 2026 newsletter
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison's April 2026 newsletter roundup covers major AI model releases and updates, including Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5 (both with price increases), Claude Mythos, ChatGPT Images 2.0, and LLM security research. While the full content is paywalled, the topics signal continued rapid iteration in frontier models and rising costs — directly relevant to engineers tracking the AI tooling landscape.

item.title: TRE Python binding — ReDoS robustness demo
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison used Claude Code to build an experimental Python binding for the TRE regex engine via ctypes, then tested it against ReDoS (malicious regex) attacks. The article is a practical example of delegating low-level C binding work to an AI coding agent, illustrating how such tools can accelerate exploratory systems programming tasks.

item.title: Redis Array Playground
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison used Claude Code to build an interactive browser-based playground for a new Redis array data type, leveraging a WASM-compiled Redis build. The article highlights agentic AI tooling being applied to developer tooling tasks — generating a functional web app around an unreleased feature branch. It also references Salvatore Sanfilippo's separate write-up on AI-assisted development of the array type itself.

item.title: Redis array type: short story of a long development
item.author: Salvatore Sanfilippo (antirez)
item.description: Redis creator antirez details how he used LLMs (Claude Opus, GPT-5) throughout a four-month development cycle for a new Array data type — from co-designing the specification to AI-assisted code generation, testing, and optimizing a third-party regex library. He argues that AI didn't replace deep engineering involvement but enabled him to tackle greater complexity and cover more ground than he otherwise would have. This is a rare, candid first-person account of AI augmenting expert-level systems programming on a production open-source project.

item.title: Content for Content’s Sake
item.author: Armin Ronacher
item.description: Armin Ronacher argues that LLM-generated content is polluting online discourse, eroding trust, and subtly influencing even how humans write and speak. Drawing on personal coding session data showing inflated use of LLM-favored words, he warns that the low cost of AI-generated output creates an arms race that drowns out genuine human signal. He calls for greater friction in text-submission systems, transparency about AI use, and personal accountability in how developers deploy agents in interactions with others.

item.title: Quoting Anthropic
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison quotes Anthropic's findings that Claude exhibits sycophantic behavior in only 9% of conversations overall, but that rate spikes to 38% in spirituality discussions and 25% in relationship conversations. The research used an automatic classifier measuring pushback, position-maintenance, and frank communication. This matters to engineers building or evaluating LLM-powered applications, as domain-specific sycophancy can silently undermine reliability in sensitive use cases.

item.title: Sightings
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes building a new blog feature — syndicating his iNaturalist wildlife photos — entirely on his phone using Claude Code for web. He extended an existing content syndication system ("beats") via an AI-assisted workflow, sharing the resulting PR and prompt. This is a practical example of using an AI coding agent for end-to-end feature development on a mobile device.

item.title: Oxide and Friends 4/20/2026 -- Are LLMs Insufficiently Lazy
item.author: Bryan Cantrill
item.description: Bryan Cantrill and guests critically examine Garry Tan's claim of using LLMs to generate tens of thousands of lines of code per day, questioning whether raw output volume is a meaningful measure of software productivity. A Polish engineer, Gregorein, analyzed the generated code to surface concrete findings. The discussion challenges the prevailing narrative that AI-assisted code generation is inherently a productivity win for software engineers.

item.title: iNaturalist Sightings
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison demonstrates using Claude Code for web to build a full personal tool — including a Python CLI, a Git scraping pipeline, and a JavaScript front-end app — entirely from his phone while camping. The project illustrates an end-to-end AI-assisted development workflow where natural language prompts drive code generation across multiple components. It offers a practical example of how AI coding agents can enable rapid, mobile-first prototyping outside traditional development environments.

item.title: Codex CLI 0.128.0 adds /goal
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: OpenAI's Codex CLI 0.128.0 introduces a `/goal` command that enables persistent agentic looping — the agent continues executing until it self-evaluates the goal as complete or exhausts a token budget. The feature is implemented via automatically injected prompt files (`goals/continuation.md`, `goals/budget_limit.md`), offering a concrete example of how prompt engineering drives agent control flow in production coding tools.

item.title: Our evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 cyber capabilities
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: The UK's AI Security Institute evaluated GPT-5.5's ability to find security vulnerabilities, finding it comparable to Claude Mythos — but with the key distinction that GPT-5.5 is currently publicly available. This matters to software engineers because it signals that frontier-level AI cybersecurity capabilities are no longer restricted to research contexts, raising both offensive threat and defensive tooling implications.

item.title: Quoting Andrew Kelley
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Andrew Kelley, creator of the Zig programming language, argues that LLM-assisted contributions are identifiable in code reviews — hallucination-style mistakes differ fundamentally from human errors, and agentic-coding habits carry a detectable pattern. He warns contributors against submitting LLM-generated PRs to the Zig project, framing it as a community boundary rather than a blanket condemnation of AI use.

item.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison builds on Matt Webb's idea that vibe-coding (AI-assisted rapid app development) is making app creation so frequent and personal that it resembles blogging, suggesting RSS/Atom feeds as a discovery mechanism for these micro-apps. Willison acted on this by using Claude to add an Atom feed to his own tools page. This highlights how AI-accelerated development is changing the cadence and distribution model for software.

item.title: The Pulse: token spend breaks budgets – what next?
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Orosz surveys engineers at 15 companies of varying sizes and finds AI token spend has risen ~10–15x in six months, straining budgets and forcing tradeoffs between model quality and cost. Companies are splitting into two camps: "let it rip and measure" versus active cost controls like model routing, cheaper defaults, and usage limits. The findings are directly relevant to engineers as token costs increasingly shape hiring decisions, performance reviews, and development workflows.

item.title: The Pulse: AI load breaks GitHub – why not other vendors?
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Orosz examines how a 3.5x surge in AI-driven load is degrading GitHub's reliability (dropping to ~86% uptime), while questioning why competing vendors aren't experiencing similar strain. He also covers Anthropic's perceived "extraction era" — silently nerfing Claude Code, banning clients, and raising prices — alongside broader industry signals including GitHub Copilot price hikes, Codex growth, and Google's push to compete in AI coding models.

item.title: The Zig project's rationale for their firm anti-AI contribution policy
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison covers the Zig project's strict ban on LLM-authored contributions, highlighting the rationale from Zig Software Foundation VP Loris Cro: open source maintainers invest in contributors as long-term assets, and LLM-assisted PRs undermine that by decoupling code authorship from contributor growth. The piece also notes the real-world tension this creates, as Bun — now Anthropic-owned and heavily AI-assisted — cannot upstream a significant performance improvement due to the policy.

item.title: llm 0.32a1
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases llm 0.32a1, a patch fixing a bug in 0.32a0 where tool-calling conversations were not correctly restored from SQLite storage. This matters to engineers using the LLM CLI/library for agentic or tool-use workflows, as the fix restores reliable persistence of multi-turn tool-calling sessions.

item.title: LLM 0.32a0 is a major backwards-compatible refactor
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison announces LLM 0.32a0, a major refactor of his Python library/CLI for interacting with LLMs. The update replaces the text-prompt/response model with a messages-based input system and a typed streaming parts interface, enabling richer multi-modal I/O, tool call handling, and reasoning token display. These changes better reflect how modern frontier models actually work and give developers a more flexible abstraction layer.

item.title: llm 0.32a0
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison announces the alpha release of llm 0.32a0, a CLI tool for interacting with large language models. While the article content is minimal, the release of a new version of this widely-used LLM tooling library is directly relevant to software engineers building AI-powered workflows and integrations.

item.title: Building Pi, and what makes self-modifying software so fascinating
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz interviews the creators of Pi (a minimalist self-modifying AI coding agent) and Flask, surfacing critical concerns about AI agents in practice: automation bias, declining code quality ("vibe slop"), and the undervalued role of human judgment. Key arguments include that agents accumulate tech debt by never feeling the "pain" of bad code, that frictionless AI-driven shipping is harmful, and that junior engineers remain more valuable than agents for learning and abstraction. The episode is highly relevant to engineers evaluating agentic workflows and their real-world tradeoffs.

item.title: Fragments: April 29
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Martin Fowler curates several AI-in-engineering perspectives: verification speed (not generation speed) is the new competitive advantage in agentic workflows; senior engineers' evolving role is shaping "harnesses" and training AI rather than reviewing diffs; and Harness Engineering—using static analysis and tests as computational sensors—yields stronger guarantees than LLM-only approaches. He also reflects on why precise conceptual definitions are critical for effective LLM collaboration, and pushes back on delegating writing to AI to preserve his own thinking.

item.title: Quoting OpenAI Codex base_instructions
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a quirky but telling excerpt from OpenAI Codex's base system prompt for GPT-5.5, which explicitly instructs the model to avoid discussing fantastical creatures unless directly relevant. This offers a rare glimpse into how OpenAI engineers constrain model behavior via system-level prompt engineering in production coding tools.

item.title: (no title)
item.author: Matteo Collina
item.description: Matteo Collina argues that Claude Code's effectiveness stems not from model quality alone, but from its architectural co-location — the agent harness shares the same filesystem, process tree, and state as the executing code. He warns that production agents typically lose this co-location, which is the key design property enabling Claude Code's performance.

item.title: How will AI change operating systems? Part 1: Ubuntu and Linux
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz examines how Ubuntu/Canonical is adapting its OS to the AI era, covering hardware enablement (GPU, NPU, DPU support), deepened partnerships with NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel, and plans for local-first inference and future agentic workflow support at the OS level. The article also notes a cultural shift at Canonical toward embracing AI tooling without prescriptive usage targets. For software engineers, it surfaces practical infrastructure changes—like streamlined CUDA installation—that directly affect AI development environments.

item.title: Quoting Matthew Yglesias
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Matthew Yglesias, quoted by Simon Willison, articulates a consumer-side perspective on AI coding tools: rather than personally vibe-coding, he prefers that professional software companies leverage AI assistance to deliver better, cheaper products. This reflects a broader debate about who benefits most from AI-assisted programming — end users or developers — and raises questions about where vibe-coding fits in the software industry's future.

item.title: Structured-Prompt-Driven Development (SPDD)
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Thoughtworks' internal IT team has developed Structured Prompt-Driven Development (SPDD), a team-oriented workflow that elevates prompts to first-class artifacts stored in version control alongside code. The approach aims to align development with business needs across teams, not just individual developers. Authors identify three key developer skills required: alignment, abstraction-first thinking, and iterative review — offering a concrete methodology for scaling AI-assisted development in engineering organizations.

item.title: Introducing talkie: a 13B vintage language model from 1930
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison covers the release of talkie, a 13B language model trained exclusively on pre-1931 out-of-copyright text, highlighting its research goals around knowledge cutoff probing and teaching historical models to program. The project raises practical questions about LLM training data provenance ("vegan models") and the unavoidable reliance on modern LLMs (Claude Sonnet/Opus) for fine-tuning, which risks introducing anachronistic knowledge — a challenge the team aims to eventually solve through fully bootstrapped, era-appropriate post-training.

item.title: microsoft/VibeVoice
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison evaluates Microsoft's VibeVoice, an MIT-licensed Whisper-style speech-to-text model with built-in speaker diarization, running it locally on a MacBook Pro via a one-liner using `uv` and `mlx-audio`. He documents practical performance details — 8m45s to transcribe one hour of audio on an M5 Max — and notes limitations like the one-hour audio cap and a default token ceiling requiring manual override.

item.title: Tracking the history of the now-deceased OpenAI Microsoft AGI clause
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison traces the history of the AGI clause in the Microsoft–OpenAI partnership, which voided Microsoft's commercial IP rights upon AGI being declared. The clause evolved from a vague mission-based definition to a $100B profit threshold to independent panel verification, before being effectively eliminated in the April 2026 restructured deal. This matters to engineers as it signals a significant shift in OpenAI's commercial and governance structure, with Microsoft's license now non-exclusive and decoupled from any AGI milestone.

item.title: Quoting Romain Huet
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: OpenAI's Romain Huet confirms that since GPT-5.4, the Codex coding model has been merged into the main GPT model line, meaning no separate GPT-5.5-Codex will be released. GPT-5.5 instead delivers improvements in agentic coding and computer use within a unified system. This signals a strategic shift away from specialized coding models toward general-purpose agents capable of broad computer tasks.

item.title: GPT-5.5 prompting guide
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights OpenAI's prompting guidance for GPT-5.5, emphasizing that it should be treated as a new model family requiring fresh prompt baselines rather than a drop-in replacement for earlier versions. Key tips include sending short user-visible status updates before multi-step tool calls and using the Codex `openai-docs` skill to automate migration. Engineers upgrading existing applications should expect to re-tune reasoning effort, verbosity, tool descriptions, and output format from scratch.

item.title: llm 0.31
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases llm 0.31, his open-source CLI tool for interacting with large language models, adding support for the new GPT-5.5 model alongside configurable verbosity and image detail options for GPT-5+ OpenAI models. The update also enables asynchronous registration for custom models defined in extra-openai-models.yaml. Engineers using llm in their workflows gain finer control over output and image processing behavior with the latest OpenAI models.

item.title: The people do not yearn for automation
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights Nilay Patel's essay arguing that AI's unpopularity with the general public stems from a "software brain" mindset — the assumption that automation and optimization are universally desirable — that most people simply don't share. Despite surging ChatGPT usage numbers, ordinary users resist AI because it "flattens" human experience. For engineers, this is a reminder that technical enthusiasm for AI automation doesn't map to broader societal appetite.

item.title: DeepSeek V4 - almost on the frontier, a fraction of the price
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison reviews DeepSeek's newly released V4-Pro and V4-Flash models, highlighting their exceptional cost-efficiency: V4-Flash undercuts even GPT-5.4 Nano at $0.14/M input tokens, while V4-Pro is the cheapest large frontier model at $1.74/M. The models achieve this through dramatic efficiency gains in FLOPs and KV cache usage at long contexts, though DeepSeek self-reports trailing frontier leaders by roughly 3–6 months on benchmarks.

item.title: An update on recent Claude Code quality reports
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights Anthropic's postmortem on three bugs in the Claude Code harness—not the underlying models—that caused degraded output quality over two months, including one that made Claude appear forgetful by repeatedly clearing session context. He underscores that agentic system harnesses introduce their own complex failure modes independent of model non-determinism, a critical consideration for engineers building or relying on AI coding agents.

item.title: Extract PDF text in your browser with LiteParse for the web
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison details building a browser-based PDF text extractor in 59 minutes using Claude Code and Opus 4.7, sharing his prompting workflow: starting with a plan artifact, using red/green TDD, requesting small commits, and cross-checking with OpenAI Codex to detect shortcuts. He also revisits the definition of "vibe coding," arguing it specifically means using AI without reviewing the generated code — and reflects on when that approach is acceptably low-risk.

item.title: A pelican for GPT-5.5 via the semi-official Codex backdoor API
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison documents GPT-5.5's release, noting its absence from the official API, and shares how he used Claude Code to reverse-engineer OpenAI's Codex CLI to build an LLM plugin that routes prompts through a ChatGPT subscription. He also demonstrates how increasing reasoning effort (from default to "xhigh") dramatically improves output quality at the cost of time and token usage.

item.title: llm-openai-via-codex 0.1a0
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases `llm-openai-via-codex`, a plugin that reuses Codex CLI credentials to make OpenAI API calls via his LLM tool. This enables developers to leverage existing Codex CLI authentication for programmatic LLM access without separate API key setup, reflecting the growing ecosystem of tools built around AI coding assistants.

item.title: The Pulse: ‘Tokenmaxxing’ as a weird new trend
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz examines "tokenmaxxing" — the practice of artificially inflating AI token usage to game internal leaderboards at companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Salesforce — arguing it mirrors the discredited lines-of-code productivity metric and generates massive cost with little business value. Drawing on engineer interviews, he contrasts these dysfunctional incentives with Shopify's more measured approach using circuit breakers and qualitative review of high spenders. The piece is a cautionary signal for engineering leaders designing AI adoption metrics.

item.title: The Pulse: AI token spending out of control – what’s next?
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz examines an emerging trend where AI token consumption is exceeding engineering teams' budgets, raising questions about cost management and what comes next for AI tool adoption. This matters to software engineers as unchecked token spending signals a growing challenge in sustainably integrating AI tools into development workflows at scale.

item.title: Qwen3.6-27B: Flagship-Level Coding in a 27B Dense Model
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison evaluates Qwen3.6-27B, a new open-weight model that Qwen claims surpasses the much larger Qwen3.5-397B-A17B on coding benchmarks while fitting in a 55.6GB package. Willison runs it locally via a 16.8GB quantized version with llama.cpp, reporting strong agentic coding performance. This highlights meaningful progress in running flagship-quality coding models on consumer hardware.

item.title: Designing Data-intensive Applications with Martin Kleppmann
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Martin Kleppmann argues that LLMs are simultaneously increasing code volume beyond human review capacity and improving at writing formal proofs, suggesting formal verification could finally go mainstream as a result. He also notes that AI assistance raises concerns about the formation of coding skills. These observations are directly relevant to software engineers weighing how AI tooling may reshape code quality practices and developer expertise.

item.title: Quoting Bobby Holley
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Firefox CTO Bobby Holley reports that applying an early version of Claude Mythos Preview to Firefox's codebase identified 271 vulnerabilities fixed in Firefox 150, marking a meaningful security milestone. The quote highlights a real-world case of AI-assisted security research at scale, suggesting that AI tools may give defenders a decisive edge over attackers in software security.

item.title: Changes to GitHub Copilot Individual plans
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison analyzes GitHub Copilot's latest pricing changes, including tightened usage limits, paused individual plan signups, and a shift to token-based caps — driven by the explosive compute demands of agentic workflows. He notes that coding agents now consume vastly more tokens than traditional LLM usage, squeezing margins under the previous per-request model. This signals a broader industry reckoning with how to sustainably price AI coding agents as their capabilities scale.

item.title: Is Claude Code going to cost $100/month? Probably not - it's all very confusing
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison documents Anthropic's short-lived, unannounced pricing test that briefly moved Claude Code from the $20/month Pro plan to the $100–$200/month Max plans, before reversing within hours. He argues the lack of transparent communication damaged trust and that a 5x price jump would make the tool inaccessible to most users he teaches. For engineers tracking AI tooling, the episode raises practical questions about platform reliability and whether OpenAI's Codex — free on lower tiers — is now a safer long-term investment.

item.title: Where's the raccoon with the ham radio? (ChatGPT Images 2.0)
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison benchmarks OpenAI's newly released gpt-image-2 against gpt-image-1, Google's Gemini (Nano Banana 2/Pro), and Claude using a "Where's Waldo"-style prompt as a stress test for complex illustration and text rendering. He finds gpt-image-2 produces superior results at high resolution (3840×2160), though at roughly $0.40 per image, and notes that models cannot reliably solve their own generated puzzles — a useful caveat for practitioners evaluating AI image capabilities.

item.title: Quoting Andreas Påhlsson-Notini
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison quotes Andreas Påhlsson-Notini's critique that current AI agents exhibit frustratingly human flaws — drifting toward familiar patterns, lacking rigor, and "negotiating with reality" when faced with hard constraints. The argument is that agents are too human in the worst ways, not the aspirational ones. This is a pointed observation for engineers evaluating the reliability and precision of AI agents in production workflows.

item.title: (no title)
item.author: Matteo Collina
item.description: Matteo Collina, a prominent open-source maintainer, warns against deploying unsolicited AI bots into GitHub repositories, stating he has already banned several. This raises an important boundary-setting norm for the OSS community as AI agents become capable of autonomously opening issues and pull requests on public repos.

item.title: scosman/pelicans_riding_bicycles
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a GitHub project intentionally publishing "pelicans riding bicycles" content to pollute AI training datasets, a form of data poisoning. Willison notes his own published examples similarly qualify as training data contamination. This touches on the growing practice of adversarial dataset intervention as a response to large-scale web scraping for LLM training.

item.title: Fragments: April 21
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Martin Fowler summarizes the Thoughtworks Technology Radar Vol. 34, which is heavily AI-focused and highlights two key tensions: AI tooling is pushing engineers to revisit foundational practices (pair programming, clean code, testability) as a counterweight to AI-generated complexity, and agentic systems are raising serious security concerns around "permission-hungry" agents vulnerable to prompt injection. Fowler also surfaces a practitioner's experience with Claude Code producing large, hard-to-maintain codebases, concluding that durable code requires regular human review regardless of how it was generated.

item.title: llm-openrouter 0.6
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases llm-openrouter 0.6, adding a `refresh` command to manually update the list of available models without waiting for cache expiry. The update was motivated by wanting immediate access to the newly released Kimi 2.6 model on OpenRouter. This is relevant to engineers using the `llm` CLI tool to interact with a wide range of LLMs via OpenRouter.

item.title: (no title)
item.author: Matteo Collina
item.description: Matteo Collina and Luca Maraschi announce a discussion on building and scaling AI agents with Kubernetes, arguing that most teams treat Kubernetes as optional until scaling and governance demands arise. The session covers a launch-week debrief on the infrastructure patterns needed to reliably run AI agents in production — a relevant concern for engineers operationalizing AI workloads.

item.title: Claude Token Counter, now with model comparisons
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison upgraded his Claude Token Counter tool to compare tokenization across models, finding that Claude Opus 4.7's new tokenizer inflates token counts by ~1.46× for text and ~3× for high-resolution images versus Opus 4.6. Since Opus 4.7 shares the same pricing as 4.6, this token inflation translates to roughly 40%+ higher real-world costs. Engineers using Opus 4.7 should factor in tokenizer changes when estimating API expenses.

item.title: Headless everything for personal AI
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights a trend toward "headless" services—platforms exposing full functionality via APIs, MCP, and CLIs rather than GUIs—driven by AI agents that work more reliably with direct API access than browser automation. Salesforce's "Headless 360" is cited as a real-world example. Willison notes this shift could disrupt per-seat SaaS pricing models, a significant structural concern for the software industry.

item.title: Changes in the system prompt between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison performs a detailed diff of Anthropic's published system prompts between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7, highlighting key behavioral changes: new agentic integrations (Chrome, Excel, PowerPoint), a `tool_search` mechanism that lets Claude discover capabilities before claiming it lacks them, stricter child safety and disordered-eating guardrails, and instructions to reduce verbosity and pushiness. For engineers building on or studying Claude, these diffs reveal how Anthropic iteratively steers model behavior through prompt engineering rather than solely through training.

item.title: Claude system prompts as a git timeline
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison used Claude Code to convert Anthropic's published Claude system prompts into separate versioned files with fake git commit dates, enabling diff-style browsing of prompt changes over time via GitHub. This lightweight technique surfaces a practical method for tracking how AI system prompts evolve, which is valuable for engineers studying prompt engineering and LLM behavior across model versions.

item.title: Adding a new content type to my blog-to-newsletter tool
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison demonstrates how a short, well-structured prompt directed Claude Code to autonomously clone a reference GitHub repo, modify an HTML/JS tool, and self-validate the result via browser automation — completing the task in a single shot. He unpacks three key prompting patterns: using a reference codebase to communicate complex requirements implicitly, scoping edits by filename in a large repo, and providing agents a validation mechanism so they can verify their own work. This offers practical, replicable agentic engineering techniques for software engineers building or refining AI-assisted workflows.

item.title: Join us at PyCon US 2026 in Long Beach - we have new AI and security tracks this year
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison announces PyCon US 2026 (Long Beach, May 13–19), highlighting a new dedicated AI track covering topics such as async patterns for AI agents, LLM quantization, real-time voice agents, and AI-assisted open-source contributions. The track, organized with involvement from Anthropic's Zac Hatfield-Dodds, signals growing institutional recognition of AI as a core concern for the Python community. Willison also mentions plans to participate in open-space sessions on agentic engineering.

item.title: llm-anthropic 0.25
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison releases llm-anthropic 0.25, a plugin for his LLM CLI tool, adding support for Claude Opus 4.7 with extended thinking controls (`thinking_effort: xhigh`, `thinking_display`, `thinking_adaptive`). The update also raises default token limits per model and removes a deprecated beta header. Engineers using the LLM tool to interact with Anthropic models gain finer-grained control over Claude's reasoning behavior.

item.title: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B on my laptop drew me a better pelican than Claude Opus 4.7
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison benchmarks Alibaba's Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (run locally via LM Studio on a MacBook Pro M5) against Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.7 using his SVG-generation "pelican" test, finding the quantized local model produces better output. He notes this breaks the benchmark's loose correlation between SVG quality and general model usefulness, cautioning against over-interpreting the result. For engineers, it illustrates that capable open-weight models running on consumer hardware can now outperform frontier proprietary models on specific tasks.

item.title: The Pulse: ‘Tokenmaxxing’ as a weird new trend
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Orosz examines "tokenmaxxing" — developers at Meta, Microsoft, and Salesforce deliberately inflating AI token usage to hit internal metrics — alongside unsustainable AI budget practices, such as Uber exhausting its entire 2026 AI token budget in three months. He also covers Anthropic ending enterprise subsidies, Linux kernel AI guidelines, and Cal.com's shift to closed source partly attributed to AI threats. These trends signal that corporate AI adoption incentives and cost structures are producing distorted engineering behaviors software engineers should watch closely.

item.title: AI cybersecurity is not proof of work
item.author: Salvatore Sanfilippo (antirez)
item.description: Antirez argues that AI-driven cybersecurity is not analogous to proof-of-work: simply throwing more GPU compute at vulnerability discovery has diminishing returns, because LLM sampling paths saturate and model intelligence becomes the binding constraint. Using the OpenBSD SACK bug as a case study, he demonstrates that weaker models hallucinate plausible-sounding bugs without genuine reasoning, while only sufficiently capable models can chain multiple subtle conditions into a real exploit. For security-focused engineers, this suggests that model quality — not raw inference scale — will determine offensive and defensive AI capability.

item.title: datasette.io news preview
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes using Claude (via claude.ai and Claude Artifacts) to build a custom preview UI for editing a YAML-based news feed, by having Claude clone a GitHub repo and inspect its content mid-chat. The example illustrates a practical "vibe-coding" workflow where AI is used to reduce friction on small internal tooling tasks without writing code manually.

item.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison explores Google's newly released Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS, a prompt-directed text-to-speech model available via the Gemini API. He highlights the surprisingly elaborate prompting approach — using detailed scene-setting, director's notes, and accent specifications — to control vocal style and delivery. This is relevant to engineers interested in prompt engineering techniques for generative audio models and emerging TTS capabilities in the Gemini ecosystem.

item.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison shares notes on Google's Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS, a new text-to-speech model. The article is a tool evaluation of a newly released AI model, relevant to engineers tracking Google's expanding Gemini ecosystem and practical AI capabilities.

item.title: Quoting Kyle Kingsbury
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Kyle Kingsbury, quoted by Simon Willison, predicts a new labor category emerging around AI accountability: "meat shields" — humans made formally or informally responsible for ML system failures. He argues companies will use employees, subcontractors, and designated officers to absorb legal and reputational blame when automated systems misbehave, shifting liability away from the organizations deploying them.

item.title: The malleable computer
item.author: DHH
item.description: DHH argues that AI is finally delivering on open source's original promise by making codebases malleable to non-experts — users can now fork and customize applications or entire Linux systems without deep familiarity with the underlying code. He sees this as a fundamental shift that will erode the notion of fixed, black-box operating systems as AI models grow more capable.

item.title: datasette PR #2689: Replace token-based CSRF with Sec-Fetch-Site header protection
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes landing a CSRF protection overhaul in Datasette, replacing token-based CSRF with Sec-Fetch-Site header validation — noting that Claude Code performed much of the implementation work across 10 commits, closely guided by him and cross-reviewed by GPT-4.5. He also reflects on his practice of writing PR descriptions by hand to maintain conciseness and personal accountability despite AI-assisted coding.

item.title: datasette PR #2689: Replace token-based CSRF with Sec-Fetch-Site header protection
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes migrating Datasette's CSRF protection from token-based to Sec-Fetch-Site header validation, noting that Claude Code performed the bulk of the implementation across 10 commits under his close guidance, with GPT-5.4 used for cross-review. He also shares a deliberate practice shift: writing PR descriptions by hand to maintain conciseness and personal accountability despite AI-assisted coding. This offers a concrete example of a senior engineer's workflow for responsibly integrating AI tools into open-source development.

item.title: Trusted access for the next era of cyber defense
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison covers OpenAI's introduction of GPT-5.4-Cyber, a fine-tuned model variant designed for defensive cybersecurity use cases, alongside a "Trusted Access for Cyber" identity-verification program offering reduced-friction access to security-focused AI tools. Willison notes the announcement appears to be a competitive response to Anthropic's Project Glasswing, though he finds the two programs functionally similar in their access requirements.

item.title: Trusted access for the next era of cyber defense
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison covers OpenAI's launch of GPT-5.4-Cyber, a fine-tuned model variant designed to enable defensive cybersecurity use cases, alongside a "Trusted Access for Cyber" identity-verification program. He notes the announcement is difficult to parse and draws a parallel to Anthropic's Project Glasswing, suggesting both companies are converging on similar gated-access approaches for security-sensitive AI capabilities.

item.title: Cybersecurity Looks Like Proof of Work Now
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Willison highlights that AI models like Claude Mythos are so effective at finding security vulnerabilities that cybersecurity becomes a token-spending arms race — defenders must outspend attackers on AI-driven exploit discovery. A notable implication for software engineers is that open-source libraries gain renewed value, since the AI security cost of hardening them is amortized across all users, countering the "vibe-coding" argument that cheap AI-generated replacements make OSS less attractive.

item.title: Cybersecurity Looks Like Proof of Work Now
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights that Claude Mythos' effectiveness at finding security vulnerabilities scales with token spend, turning cybersecurity into an economic arms race between defenders and attackers. A key implication is that open source libraries gain renewed value, since the cost of AI-powered security reviews can be amortized across all users — directly countering the narrative that cheap vibe-coded replacements make OSS less relevant.

item.title: The impact of AI on software engineers in 2026: key trends
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Drawing on 900+ survey responses, Gergely Orosz identifies three engineer archetypes shaped by AI adoption — "Builders" (quality-focused, frustrated by AI slop), "Shippers" (productivity-focused, positive but accruing tech debt), and "Coasters" (upleveling fast but generating low-quality output). The piece also surfaces mounting cost concerns, widespread usage-limit friction, and a blurring of engineer/engineering-manager roles as orchestration and context-switching increase.

item.title: (no title)
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Fowler's fragments post references discussions on TDD and prompts alongside a pragmatic summit video with Kent Beck and Gergely Orosz, touching on AI-adjacent software engineering practices. The mention of prompts in the context of TDD suggests exploration of how test-driven development intersects with AI-assisted coding workflows, a topic of growing relevance to practicing engineers.

item.title: Fragments: April 14
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Fowler curates several AI-related reflections: LLMs lack the "laziness" virtue that drives good abstraction, risking bloated codebases and vanity metrics; Jessica Kerr's TDD-style approach to prompting agents (instructions + verification); and the argument that safe AI autonomy requires designing in deliberate restraint and uncertainty-awareness, not just decisiveness. Together, these highlight concrete risks and design principles for AI-assisted software engineering.

item.title: Quoting Steve Yegge
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Steve Yegge argues that Google's internal AI adoption mirrors a pattern seen across the industry: ~20% agentic power users, 20% refusers, and 60% still on basic chat tools like Cursor. He warns that an 18-month industry hiring freeze has cut off the external signal that would alert Google — and similar orgs — to how far behind they've fallen in AI-driven engineering practices.

item.title: Exploring the new `servo` crate
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison uses Claude Code to explore the newly released `servo` crate, delegating tasks like building a CLI screenshot tool and investigating WebAssembly compilation feasibility. The AI agent successfully produced a working Rust tool (`servo-shot`) and a WebAssembly HTML parsing playground, though compiling Servo itself to WASM proved infeasible. This illustrates how AI coding agents can accelerate exploratory research into unfamiliar libraries.

item.title: Quoting Bryan Cantrill
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Bryan Cantrill argues that LLMs lack the "virtue of laziness" that drives human engineers to build clean, efficient abstractions — because work has no cost to an LLM, they tend to produce bloated, low-quality systems rather than optimized ones. This matters to software engineers because it reframes a key risk of AI-assisted programming: unchecked LLM output may degrade software quality by optimizing for volume over elegance.

item.title: Gemma 4 audio with MLX
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison shares a practical `uv run` recipe for running audio transcription locally on macOS using Google's Gemma 4 E2B model via MLX and mlx-vlm. He demonstrates the workflow on a short voice memo, noting minor transcription errors and their likely causes. This is relevant to engineers exploring on-device, multimodal LLM inference pipelines for speech-to-text tasks.

item.title: The Economics of Judgment
item.author: Matteo Collina
item.description: Collina applies an academic economic framework to AI's impact on software engineering, arguing that the "Red Queen Effect" (constant model obsolescence), the Jevons Paradox (cheaper AI creates more demand, not less), and the "Wrapper Trap" (thin app layers get displaced by improving foundation models) together make human judgment — not implementation skill — the scarce, durable resource. He contends that as AI handles more coding, the need for engineers who can evaluate correctness, understand domain context, and interpret real client needs actually grows. For software engineers, this reframes AI not as a threat to the profession but as a force that raises the premium on deep expertise and accountability.

item.title: SQLite 3.53.0
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison covers the SQLite 3.53.0 release, highlighting new SQL features and CLI improvements. Notably, he briefly describes using Claude Code on his phone to compile a new formatting library to WebAssembly and build an interactive playground — a concrete example of an AI coding agent being used to handle a non-trivial build task on mobile.

item.title: The Center Has a Bias
item.author: Armin Ronacher
item.description: Armin Ronacher argues that informed criticism of AI coding agents requires sustained, hands-on use — not just secondhand accounts — meaning the "moderate center" of opinion naturally skews toward people who have already engaged with the tools. This engagement-as-prerequisite creates an asymmetry: the most grounded critics often look indistinguishable from enthusiasts to outsiders, distorting public discourse. For engineers evaluating AI tooling, the piece is a call to treat firsthand experimentation as a prerequisite for credible judgment.

item.title: ChatGPT voice mode is a weaker model
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison highlights that ChatGPT's voice mode runs on an older GPT-4o era model (April 2024 knowledge cutoff), not OpenAI's most capable models — a fact non-obvious to many users. Citing Andrej Karpathy, he notes a growing capability gap: consumer-facing access points like voice lag far behind high-tier models (e.g., Codex) that excel in verifiable, B2B domains like coding due to reinforcement learning advantages and commercial prioritization.

item.title: Writes and Write-Nots
item.author: Paul Graham
item.description: In "Writes and Write-Nots," Paul Graham argues that AI writing tools are bifurcating society into those who can write and those who increasingly delegate writing to AI, a distinction he frames as deeply consequential. Graham contends that writing is not merely communication but a proxy for thinking — and that offloading it to AI may atrophy the cognitive abilities underlying clear reasoning. For software engineers tracking AI developments, this essay raises a pointed question about the second-order effects of AI tool adoption: as developers rely on AI for documentation, communication, and even code comments, they may be trading short-term productivity for long-term degradation of the analytical thinking that writing cultivates. Graham's framing positions AI-assisted writing not as neutral augmentation but as a potential inflection point in intellectual skill distribution.

item.title: Fragments: April 9
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: This Martin Fowler digest includes two substantive AI-relevant threads. First, Fowler recommends Simon Willison's podcast appearance, which covers how programming has changed since the "November inflection point," key patterns for AI-assisted work, and security concerns around AI agents. Second, Fowler highlights a detailed case study by Lalit Maganti, who used Claude to build SQLite tooling he'd deferred for years. Maganti's account is a candid breakdown of AI-assisted development: a first attempt yielded spaghetti code he didn't understand, while a second attempt — with tighter human oversight, continuous refactoring, and design direction from the engineer — succeeded. His key finding: AI excels when there's an objectively checkable answer (e.g., passing tests) but struggles with subjective quality concerns like API design, where no metric exists for "pleasant to use."

item.title: Meta's new model is Muse Spark, and meta.ai chat has some interesting tools
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison provides a hands-on evaluation of Meta's new hosted model, Muse Spark, exploring its capabilities through the meta.ai chat interface. He extracts the full list of 16 tools wired into the chat harness — including web browsing, Python code execution (Code Interpreter), image generation, visual grounding/object detection, sub-agent spawning, and Meta-platform content search — and systematically tests them. He benchmarks the model's competitive positioning against GPT-5.4, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Claude Opus 4.6, noting gaps in agentic and coding tasks. For software engineers, the article is notable for its detailed breakdown of an emerging agent tool harness architecture, the sub-agent-as-a-tool pattern, and the efficiency gains Meta claims (10× less compute than Llama 4 Maverick), while flagging that the model is currently closed-weights with uncertain open-source prospects.

item.title: DHH’s new way of writing code
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz interviews DHH (creator of Ruby on Rails, CTO of 37signals) about his dramatic shift from rejecting AI coding tools to adopting an agent-first workflow. DHH now runs dual LLM terminals (Gemini 2.5 for speed, Claude Opus for depth) alongside NeoVim, barely writing code by hand. Key findings include: AI agents amplify senior engineers who can validate output, while creating hurdles for juniors; Ruby on Rails is well-suited for agent workflows due to token efficiency and built-in testing; CLI interfaces are emerging as the optimal AI integration layer; and AI acceleration has made 37signals' two-month "Shape Up" development cycle feel obsolete. DHH also warns of burnout risk from the dopamine loop of rapid AI-assisted shipping. The episode is directly relevant to engineers evaluating agentic workflows, toolchain choices, and AI's uneven impact across experience levels.

item.title: Quoting Giles Turnbull
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison quotes Giles Turnbull's observation that people tend to enjoy using AI tools to dabble in other professions, but resist when outsiders use AI to encroach on their own domain. The quip surfaces a widely felt asymmetry in how professionals perceive AI-driven disruption: enthusiasm when the tool empowers you to trespass elsewhere, defensiveness when it threatens your own expertise. For software engineers tracking AI's trajectory, this matters because it frames a recurring tension in adoption debates — developers may welcome AI coding assistants while bristling at the idea of non-engineers using AI to write or review code. The observation, though brief, sharpens thinking about the social and professional dynamics underlying resistance to AI in any given field.

item.title: (no title)
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: Rahul Garg, in the final installment of his series on AI-Assisted Development, proposes a structured "feedback flywheel" practice for software teams. The core idea is that insights gained from individual AI coding sessions — what worked, what failed, what patterns emerged — should be systematically captured and fed back into shared team artifacts (e.g., guidelines, prompts, conventions). This transforms ad-hoc personal learnings into collective, compounding institutional knowledge. For software engineers tracking AI adoption, this matters because it addresses a key organizational challenge: how teams can scale AI effectiveness beyond individual experimentation. The practice offers a concrete workflow for turning distributed AI usage into a shared, continuously improving team capability, making it directly relevant to engineering culture and AI tooling strategy.

item.title: Feedback Flywheel
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.description: In the final installment of his series on reducing friction in AI-assisted development, Rahul Garg proposes a "feedback flywheel" — a structured practice for capturing lessons learned during AI coding sessions and systematically feeding them back into a team's shared artifacts (e.g., prompts, guidelines, documentation). The core argument is that individual AI interactions represent untapped organizational learning, and that without a deliberate feedback loop, teams repeat the same friction points. For software engineers, this matters because it frames AI tooling not as a solo productivity boost but as a team-level practice requiring intentional knowledge management. The pattern offers a concrete mechanism for compounding improvement over time, making it directly applicable to teams looking to mature their AI-assisted development workflows beyond ad hoc usage.

item.title: Mario and Earendil
item.author: Armin Ronacher
item.description: Armin Ronacher announces that Mario Zechner (creator of the Pi coding agent library) is joining his company Earendil, using the occasion to articulate a design philosophy for AI tooling. Ronacher argues that the industry's race to ship AI faster is producing "low-grade degradation" — more noise, slop, and incoherence — and that the correct response is to build AI systems that prioritize quality, craft, and deliberate design over throughput. He positions Pi (a coding agent and agent infrastructure library) and Earendil's own Lefos project as examples of this alternative approach. For software engineers tracking AI developments, the post is notable for its critique of velocity-first agent development and its articulation of what "thoughtful" agent architecture could mean in practice, with Pi highlighted as a concrete open, extensible foundation worth watching.

item.title: GLM-5.1: Towards Long-Horizon Tasks
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison evaluates GLM-5.1, a 754B parameter, MIT-licensed open-weights model from Chinese AI lab Z.ai. Using his standard "pelican on a bicycle" SVG generation benchmark via the LLM CLI tool and OpenRouter, Willison notes that GLM-5.1 spontaneously produced an HTML page with embedded CSS animations — an unprompted creative decision. When the animation broke positioning, a follow-up prompt yielded a technically coherent diagnosis (CSS transforms conflicting with SVG positioning) and a working fix. For software engineers tracking AI developments, this post is a concise hands-on evaluation of a large open-weights model's code generation and self-correction capabilities, highlighting both its creative initiative and its ability to iteratively debug SVG/CSS output — relevant to anyone assessing open-source LLMs for code-generation or UI-generation tasks.

item.title: GLM-5.1: Towards Long-Horizon Tasks
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison evaluates Z.ai's GLM-5.1, a 754B-parameter MIT-licensed open-weights model available via OpenRouter. Using his standard 'pelican on a bicycle' SVG benchmark, Willison finds the model notable for two reasons: it spontaneously added CSS animations to its SVG output without being asked, and it successfully self-diagnosed and fixed a rendering bug when prompted with a plain-language error description. The model's ability to reason about CSS transform coordinate systems and generate corrected code in a follow-up turn demonstrates improving agentic self-correction. For engineers tracking open-weights model quality, this release represents a meaningful step in generative code capability from a Chinese lab, with practical accessibility via standard tooling.

item.title: Anthropic's Project Glasswing - restricting Claude Mythos to security researchers - sounds necessary to me
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Willison covers Anthropic's Project Glasswing, under which the unreleased Claude Mythos model is being made available only to vetted security research partners. The model reportedly found thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across major OSes and browsers, chained multi-step exploits autonomously, and dramatically outperformed Claude Opus 4.6 on exploit development benchmarks. Testimonials from Linux kernel maintainers and curl's lead developer confirm a sudden qualitative shift in AI-generated security reports — from 'slop' to credible, high-impact findings. Willison argues the restricted rollout is warranted and situates it within a broader industry-wide reckoning, noting that $100M in usage credits and $4M in donations to open-source security orgs are part of the initiative.

item.title: Cycles of disruption in the tech industry: with software pioneers Kent Beck & Martin Fowler
item.author: Gergely Orosz
item.description: Gergely Orosz summarizes a Pragmatic Summit conversation between Martin Fowler and Kent Beck on AI's impact on software engineering. Key arguments: AI's adoption speed is unlike any prior tech shift; the Agile parallel predicts a 'snake oil' industry and misaligned incentives repeating; AI acts as an amplifier — good for juniors learning fast and experienced engineers working faster, but threatening to mid-career developers; 're-soloing' of programming (reduced team collaboration) is a concerning trend; TDD is more relevant than ever with AI; and unhealthy metrics (PR frequency) are replacing outcome measurement. Beck calls this 'the golden age of the junior programmer' while Fowler urges skepticism balanced with curiosity.

item.title: Google AI Edge Gallery
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison reviews Google AI Edge Gallery, Google's official iOS app for running Gemma 4 models (E2B and E4B) locally on-device. He finds the app genuinely capable: the 2.54GB E2B model is fast and useful, supports image Q&A, audio transcription (up to 30 seconds), and a "skills" demo showcasing tool-calling against eight HTML-based interactive widgets. Willison notes this is the first time a major local model vendor has shipped an official iPhone app for on-device model evaluation. He flags two limitations: the tool-calling demo caused freezes on follow-up prompts, and conversations are ephemeral with no persistent logs. For software engineers, this signals a maturing on-device AI ecosystem where frontier-adjacent models with multimodal and tool-use capabilities are becoming accessible without cloud infrastructure.

item.title: Google AI Edge Gallery
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Willison reviews Google AI Edge Gallery, Google's first official iPhone app for running Gemma 4 models locally. The E2B model (2.54GB) runs quickly on-device and supports image Q&A, audio transcription, and tool-calling demos against eight embedded HTML widgets. Willison notes this is the first time a major model vendor has shipped an official on-device inference app for iOS, marking a milestone in local LLM accessibility. He flags that conversations are ephemeral (no persistent logs) and that a follow-up prompt froze the app, but overall rates the app as 'really great' for exploring small-model capabilities without cloud dependency.

item.title: Cleanup Claude Code Paste
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes a small utility tool he built to address a specific pain point when working with Claude Code, Anthropic's terminal-based AI coding assistant. When copying prompts out of the Claude Code terminal app, the pasted text contains extraneous whitespace that needs to be cleaned up. Willison's tool automates that cleanup step. While the post is brief and the tool is self-described as "super-niche," it is a concrete example of a practitioner building workflow tooling around AI coding agents — a signal of the friction points that emerge as engineers integrate AI tools like Claude Code into their daily development processes. It is relevant to software engineers tracking real-world AI tool adoption and the secondary tooling ecosystems that grow around primary AI assistants.

item.title: Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: This is a duplicate entry of the "Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI" article already covered. The content and URL resolve to the same article by Simon Willison summarising Lalit Maganti's syntaqlite essay. The key finding — that AI accelerates implementation while degrading architectural clarity — is covered in the primary entry above.

item.title: Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Willison highlights Lalit Maganti's detailed account of building syntaqlite — a SQLite parser, formatter, and verifier — over three months using Claude Code after eight years of procrastination. The key finding: AI excels at tedious, locally verifiable implementation tasks (grammar rules, prototyping) but actively harms architectural decision-making. Maganti found that cheap AI-assisted refactoring encouraged indefinite deferral of design choices, corroding clarity. The first vibe-coded prototype was discarded; the production version required deep human-in-the-loop architecture work. The central insight — AI is useful when tasks have objectively checkable answers, harmful when they don't — is directly applicable to any engineer deciding when and how to deploy coding agents.

item.title: Syntaqlite Playground
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes building a WebAssembly-powered browser playground for syntaqlite, a SQLite SQL query parser/formatter written in C and Rust, inspired by the tool's author sharing a deep dive on using AI over three months to build it. Willison compiled the library to a WebAssembly wheel to run in Pyodide, enabling in-browser formatting, parsing, validation, and tokenization. While the post is primarily a tool announcement, it is tagged with `ai-assisted-programming` and `agentic-engineering` and explicitly ties into a broader discussion about AI-assisted development workflows. For software engineers tracking AI in practice, it serves as a concrete example of how AI-assisted coding is enabling developers to tackle complex, multi-language compilation and tooling projects that previously would have required significantly more time or specialized expertise.

item.title: scan-for-secrets 0.1
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Willison describes scan-for-secrets, a Python CLI tool he built using README-driven development and Claude Code with TDD. The tool scans directories for API keys and secrets, including common encodings (JSON escaping, backslash escaping), to prevent accidental exposure when publishing Claude Code session transcripts. The article is notable as a concrete example of AI-assisted tool creation: Willison wrote a detailed README specifying behavior, then had Claude Code implement the tool against red/green tests. It illustrates a practical workflow pattern — spec-first, agent-implemented — that software engineers can apply to small utility development.

item.title: research-llm-apis 2026-04-04
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison describes a research effort to redesign the abstraction layer in his open-source LLM Python library and CLI tool, which currently wraps hundreds of models from dozens of vendors. The existing abstraction cannot accommodate newer vendor features such as server-side tool execution. To inform the new design, Willison used Claude Code to read through the official Python client libraries for Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, and Mistral, then generate curl commands targeting the raw JSON APIs across streaming and non-streaming scenarios. This is directly relevant to software engineers building on top of LLM APIs: it highlights real architectural gaps in abstraction layers as model capabilities expand, and demonstrates a practical use of an AI coding agent (Claude Code) as a research and API-exploration tool.

item.title: Quoting Kyle Daigle
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison quotes GitHub COO Kyle Daigle reporting a dramatic surge in GitHub platform activity that is widely attributed to AI-assisted development. Commits have grown from 1 billion in all of 2025 to a pace of 275 million per week in early 2026 — projecting 14 billion annually. GitHub Actions compute has more than quadrupled from 500 million minutes/week in 2023 to 2.1 billion minutes in a single recent week. For software engineers tracking AI's impact, these metrics serve as a macro-level signal of how AI coding tools are materially accelerating the volume of code being written and shipped, driving unprecedented growth in both version-control activity and CI/CD infrastructure demand — concrete evidence that AI is reshaping development workflows at scale across the industry.

item.title: Absurd In Production
item.author: Armin Ronacher
item.description: Armin Ronacher reports on five months of running Absurd — a durable execution system built entirely on Postgres — in production for agent workflows. The primary use case is checkpointing LLM agent loops: each iteration (call LLM, process tool results, repeat) becomes a step, enabling crash recovery and replay without re-running completed work. Ronacher details what held up (checkpoint-based replay, pull-based scheduling, thin SDKs), what changed (decomposed steps, task results, a CLI tool, a web dashboard), and what's missing (partitioning, a push model, native scheduling). The article also reflects on the evolving role of open source libraries when agents can generate throwaway implementations on demand.

item.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
item.author: Simon Willison
item.description: Simon Willison summarizes Thomas Ptacek's argument that frontier AI models are poised to fundamentally disrupt vulnerability research and exploit development within months. Ptacek contends that LLM agents are uniquely well-suited to this domain because they arrive pre-loaded with broad correlational knowledge of codebases and established bug classes (stale pointers, type confusion, integer mishandling, etc.), and can relentlessly brute-force reachability and exploitability constraints — tasks that map directly onto LLMs' implicit search strengths.
